CHS Alumni Weekly Newsletter

Season 2025-26 | Week 21 (Mar 16 to Mar 22)
Matchup Summaries Report Cards Betting Lines Player of the Week Fun Facts What If? Playoff Championship Odds Stats Corner Around the NBA Rumor Mill Record Book

Matchup Summaries

Luka my Balls 1643.45 def. Smaxey 1583.50

Luka my Balls (17-4): 45 games, 36.52 FPPG (G: 40.6 | F: 32.1 | C: 35.1)

Smaxey (10-11): 42 games, 37.70 FPPG (G: 38.3 | F: 36.0 | C: 40.4)

Season series: Nick 5, Benton 2 | All-time: Nick leads 37-28

The regular season closed with exactly the kind of result that makes a playoff bracket feel unstable before it even starts. Nick came in as a +120 underdog on the moneyline and was getting 33.0 points on the spread, then went out and beat Benton by 59.95 anyway. That is not a squeaker or a lucky bounce. That is a controlled upset in the final tune-up before the semifinals, and it pushes Luka my Balls into Week 22 on a four-game winning streak while Smaxey arrives having dropped four straight. For a league about timing as much as talent, that matters.

Luka Doncic (257.35 FP on 4 games) was the obvious engine, and the 89.8 FP eruption at Miami on 3/19 was the sort of supernova performance that bends an entire matchup around one player. But this was not a one-man heist. Nickeil Alexander-Walker (163.05 FP on 4 games) and Amen Thompson (159.70 FP on 4 games) gave Nick the secondary scoring Benton could not quite match, and that depth is what kept the week from becoming a pure star-for-star duel. Nick only needed enough support around Luka to keep the scoreboard moving, and he got more than enough.

What made the matchup interesting is that Benton was hardly flat. James Harden (165.00 FP on 3 games), Trey Murphy III (162.95 FP on 4 games), and Jaylen Brown (162.20 FP on 4 games) all turned in strong weeks, and on a per-game basis Smaxey still held the edge at forward and center. The problem was that Nick won the guard battle outright at 40.6 FPPG to 38.3 and also banked a three-game volume edge, which gave him more possessions, more outs, and more room for Luka to break the week open. Benton lost 11 games to injury in the regular-season finale, and once the matchup tilted early, there was not enough roster elasticity left to recover.

So the final accounting is clean and a little ominous for the two seed. Nick takes the season series 5-2, extends the all-time edge to 37-28, and heads into the playoffs looking like the one team nobody wanted to see get hot at the right moment. Benton still finished with the No. 2 seed, but the regular season did not end with a statement of dominance. It ended with another reminder that the bracket may say one thing and current form may say another.

Saboner 1626.70 def. Big Nik Energy 1564.50

Big Nik Energy (5-16): 45 games, 34.77 FPPG (G: 37.1 | F: 29.7 | C: 38.2)

Saboner (10-11): 45 games, 36.15 FPPG (G: 32.0 | F: 37.2 | C: 42.4)

Season series: Garrett 6, Hayden 1 | All-time: Hayden leads 34-31

Garrett did not need a miracle here. He just needed his roster to look like itself, and in the regular-season finale it absolutely did. Saboner beat Big Nik Energy 1626.70--1564.50 in a matchup where both teams played 45 games, which means the difference came down less to schedule luck and more to raw lineup quality over the course of the week. That is a useful signal heading into the playoffs. Garrett was already projected as the stronger side coming in, and this one played out like a team sharpening its edge before the bracket opens.

The center of the story was, appropriately, center. Victor Wembanyama (189.60 FP on 4 games), Donovan Clingan (170.60 FP on 4 games), and Rudy Gobert (163.65 FP on 4 games) gave Garrett a brutally efficient interior core, and the positional numbers tell the rest: Saboner posted 42.4 FPPG at center and 37.2 at forward, both comfortably ahead of Hayden’s marks. In a 45-games-to-45-games matchup, that kind of frontcourt gap is hard to survive, because it is not one bad night or one random dud. It is a structural advantage repeated across the entire week.

Hayden still got exactly the sort of star work that keeps him dangerous once the playoffs begin. Nikola Jokic (201.15 FP on 4 games) was again the anchor, LaMelo Ball (156.95 FP on 3 games) supplied real punch, and Devin Booker (149.45 FP on 5 games) kept the backcourt competitive enough to avoid a total collapse. But 29.7 FPPG from the forward group left too much missing offense, and that is where Garrett steadily separated. Hayden’s top-end talent kept the game respectable; Garrett’s balance won it.

The result puts a sharp finishing touch on one of the most lopsided season series in the league this year. Garrett takes it 6-1 despite still trailing Hayden 34-31 all-time, which says just as much about the weirdness of this season as it does about either manager’s history. The playoffs start now, and that reset is especially important here: Garrett enters as the No. 3 seed with the best championship odds in the field, while Hayden enters as the No. 4 seed with a losing record and yet a very real chance to wreck the bracket. Even in defeat, Big Nik Energy still feels dangerous. In victory, Saboner looks like the team that has been waiting all season for the games to matter most.

Report Cards

Garrett (Saboner) C+

Record: 10-11 | Won vs Hayden by 62.20 | 1626.70 FP | -2.8% vs projection | Injuries: 11 | Bench: 0 | Blunders: 0 | Waivers: 2 adds (98.35 FP over 5 games, 19.67 FPPG, 49.2 FP per add)

This is a solid C+ because Garrett did not do anything flashy, but he did almost everything useful. He was at -2.8% versus projection, so this was not a week where the roster wildly outperformed expectations. It was a week where the stars were stable, the build held together, and the matchup never really slipped out of his control. Wembanyama, Clingan, and Gobert gave him a dominant core, and that frontcourt advantage carried the whole result even without an efficiency spike. The zeroes in the bench and blunder columns matter too, because playoff weeks are often decided by whether a manager quietly gives points away. Garrett also navigated 11 games lost to injury, which fits a season-long story of a 24.7% injury burden, 169 games lost to injury, and another 130 IL games. The win itself is not enough to make the grade soar, but the shape of it matters: clean process, no self-sabotage, good big-man production, and a roster that still looks like the most dangerous one in the bracket.

Benton (Smaxey) C

Record: 10-11 | Lost to Nick by 59.95 | 1583.50 FP | -2.4% vs projection | Injuries: 11 | Bench: 1 | Blunders: 1 (47.9 FP wasted) | Waivers: 0 adds

This lands at a plain C because the week was not a disaster in pure scoring terms, but it was a bad combination of bad timing and avoidable damage. Benton still posted 1583.50 FP, which is enough to win plenty of weeks in this league, and the healthy starters were only -2.4% versus projection. Harden, Murphy, and Brown all gave him credible production, so this was not a case of the whole roster falling apart. The problem is that 11 games were lost to injury and the one thing he absolutely could control went the wrong way. Leaving LeBron James’ 47.9 FP on the bench while Cade Cunningham’s UTIL slot sat empty is exactly the kind of mistake that looks survivable in February and unforgivable once the playoffs begin. It also lands on top of a season injury burden of 25.9%, with 175 games lost to injury and 138 more parked in IL, so there is no extra margin anywhere in the build. Benton still owns the No. 2 seed, but four straight losses to close the regular season have changed the feel of his team completely. The grade reflects that tension: respectable raw output, shaky management, and very little evidence that the slide has actually stopped.

Nick (Luka my Balls) C

Record: 17-4 | Won vs Benton by 59.95 | 1643.45 FP | -3.2% vs projection | Injuries: 7 | Bench: 1 | Blunders: 0 | Waivers: 3 adds (297.95 FP over 9 games, 33.11 FPPG, 99.3 FP per add)

This is one of those wins where the grade and the result tell slightly different stories. Nick won by 59.95 points and extended the streak to four, but the roster itself still checked in at -3.2% versus projection, which is why the grade stays at C instead of climbing. The headline was obviously Luka’s 257.35 FP, yet the more useful supporting detail might be the waiver wire, where three adds produced 297.95 FP over 9 games and effectively patched over the weak spots. That kind of in-season roster maintenance is part of why Nick keeps surviving weeks that are not especially efficient on paper. He also only lost 7 games to injury this week, and the bigger season picture is even friendlier: a league-low 19.7% injury burden, 144 games lost to injury, and 93 IL games. That has given him more week-to-week stability than anyone else, and it shows in the 17-4 record. So the grade is ordinary, but the position is not. Nick did not need to be perfect in the regular-season finale; he needed to win, protect the momentum, and get into the playoffs with Luka looking terrifying. He accomplished all of that.

Hayden (Big Nik Energy) C

Record: 5-16 | Lost to Garrett by 62.20 | 1564.50 FP | -1.7% vs projection | Injuries: 6 | Bench: 1 | Blunders: 0 | Waivers: 2 adds (136.90 FP over 5 games, 27.38 FPPG, 68.5 FP per add)

Hayden’s report card is a good reminder that not every loss is sloppy. He was actually the most efficient manager of the week at just -1.7% versus projection, which means Big Nik Energy generally got what it should have from the healthy pieces. Jokic was still Jokic, the waiver adds were competent rather than empty calories, and the lineup avoided the sort of blunder that can turn a respectable week into a self-inflicted mess. The issue was simply that respectable was not enough against Garrett’s frontcourt avalanche. Hayden only lost 6 games to injury in Week 21, but the broader season context remains brutal: a 26.6% injury burden, 184 games lost to injury, and another 133 IL games shaping almost every week of the season. That is a big part of how a roster with this much top-end firepower ends up 5-16 entering the playoffs. The grade stays at C because the process was fine and the ceiling just was not there. And that is what makes Hayden dangerous now: the record says dead weight, but the week itself looked more like a team waiting for one bracket-breaking Jokic series.

Betting Lines

Luka my Balls vs Big Nik Energy
Team Spread Total Moneyline Win Prob Avg Score
Luka my Balls +118.5 o3730 +190 34.62% 1810.84
Big Nik Energy -118.5 u3730 -190 65.38% 1917.31

This is the strangest semifinal on the board: the No. 1 seed is catching 118.5 points and sitting at +190 on the moneyline. The marquee matchup is Luka Doncic at 57.0 projected FPPG against Nikola Jokic at 62.2, with the two league-shaping superstars set to decide whether the bracket follows seed or projection. Big Nik Energy gets the one-game schedule edge and enters largely intact outside Lauri Markkanen being done for the year, which is why the four seed is favored despite the 5-16 record. Nick’s counter is a massive projected edge at forward and the possibility of midweek reinforcements, with Jalen Johnson and Brandon Ingram potentially back by Wednesday and Anthony Edwards a possible late-week boost. Luka my Balls has won six straight against Big Nik Energy, but the regular season is over now; this is an elimination game, and the one seed has to prove the standings still matter.

Smaxey vs Saboner
Team Spread Total Moneyline Win Prob Avg Score
Smaxey +194.5 o3020 +325 23.50% 1424.50
Saboner -194.5 u3020 -325 76.50% 1599.33

This line is brutal, and the number explains why: Saboner is laying 194.5 points and sitting at -325 on the moneyline in what projects as the least competitive semifinal. The headliner is James Harden at 43.5 projected FPPG against Victor Wembanyama at 55.5, a gap that captures the difference between Benton's patchwork ceiling and Garrett’s current star power. Smaxey is down to 38 startable games, a seven-game deficit, with Cade Cunningham done for the season and both Tyrese Maxey and Trae Young still expected to miss this week. Garrett is not fully healthy either, with Domantas Sabonis out for the year and Giannis Antetokounmpo still about a week away, but Immanuel Quickley could be back by Wednesday and the center matchup still tilts heavily his way. Benton did win the regular-season series 4-3, yet none of that changes the Week 22 math: this is an elimination game, and Garrett enters it as the clearest favorite on the board.

Player of the Week

Luka Doncic

257.35 FP over 4 games (64.34 FPPG) | +12.87% vs projection | 15.66% of team output

The playoffs do not officially begin until Week 22, but Luka clearly decided to get started early. His 257.35 FP across 4 games led the league, and the 89.8 FP masterpiece at Miami on March 19 was the week’s defining performance. More than that, the timing could not have been better: Nick needed a statement win heading into the bracket, and Luka gave him one. This is Doncic’s fourth Player of the Week award of the season, joining Weeks 6, 15, and 18, and it pulls Nick up to 6 total POTWs on the year. Hayden still leads that race with 7, but with the postseason here, Luka is the player nobody wants to see on the other side of a semifinal.

Honorable Mentions: Nikola Jokic put up 201.15 FP over 4 games (50.29 FPPG) and still somehow finished a distant second, while Victor Wembanyama posted 189.60 FP over 4 games (47.40 FPPG) to keep Garrett’s late-season surge rolling. The stars are lined up exactly the way a playoff bracket should want them.

Fun Facts

  • Garrett is now just 1 win behind Benton for No. 3 on the all-time wins leaderboard, which gives the 3-vs-2 semifinal a little extra historical bite before a single playoff point is scored.
  • Luka Doncic's 89.8 FP detonation at Miami was the loudest single-game performance of the week, and it arrived at exactly the right moment for a Nick team trying to carry regular-season momentum into the bracket.
  • Benton has now lost 4 straight, which matches his season-worst skid and sends the No. 2 seed into the playoffs with the worst possible kind of momentum.
  • Nick's guards outscored Benton's by 277 FP in Week 21, a swing big enough to decide the matchup even before Luka's nuclear week is layered on top.
  • Benton's forwards still outscored Nick's by 169 FP, which is part of what made that matchup feel so weird: Smaxey won a major frontcourt battle and still lost the week by nearly 60.
  • Hayden has dealt with 317 total injury games this season, which is 80 more than Nick's league-low 237, and it is a big reason a roster this talented finished 5-16 before suddenly becoming a dangerous playoff spoiler.
  • Nick's 61.5% career win rate still sets the league standard, leading Hayden by 9.7 percentage points and reinforcing why the No. 1 seed still carries historical gravity even if the Week 22 odds do not.

What If?

  • Benton committed 1 blunder: LeBron James (47.9 FP) sat on the bench while Cade Cunningham's UTIL slot was wide open on 3/19. That is 47.9 completely free points left on the table in a matchup Benton ultimately lost by 59.95.
  • Benton also left 58.7 potential points on the bench overall, the biggest missed-gain figure of the week. For a team entering the semifinals on a 4-game losing streak, that is the kind of detail that sticks.
  • Nick left 28.8 potential points on the bench, but none of it rose to the level of a true blunder and none of it would have changed the outcome.
  • No optimal lineup would have flipped either Week 21 matchup. Nick, Hayden, and Garrett all managed clean weeks with 0 blunders, while Benton was the only manager to leave truly avoidable points exposed. In the regular season, that is frustrating. In the playoffs, it is lethal. Setting the lineup is not optional.

Playoff Championship Odds

Playoff Championship Probability

Team Champ % Runner-Up % 3rd % 4th %
Saboner 47.3% 28.9% 14.5% 9.3%
Big Nik Energy 21.4% 43.6% 9.6% 25.4%
Smaxey 16.8% 7.0% 52.5% 23.7%
Luka my Balls 14.6% 20.5% 23.4% 41.6%

Power Rankings

Rank Team Record Playoff Odds Trend
1 Saboner 10-11 47.3% ^
2 Big Nik Energy 5-16 21.4% ^
3 Smaxey 10-11 16.8% v
4 Luka my Balls 17-4 14.6% v

The bracket is upside down before a single semifinal tip. The No. 4 seed, Big Nik Energy, is favored over the No. 1 seed at 64.9% to 35.1%, and the No. 3 seed, Saboner, is an even heavier favorite over the No. 2 seed at 76.2% to 23.8%. The most likely championship matchup is Garrett vs Hayden at 49.5% likelihood, which means the two teams with the worst regular-season records are now the most likely finalists in the playoff tournament. That is the cleanest possible reminder that the regular season is over and the math has reset.

Saboner sits on top because the path is simply the cleanest. Garrett enters Week 22 with the best championship odds at 47.3%, is averaging 1737.5 over the last 3 weeks against a 1535.8 season average, and draws a Benton roster that is down to 71.7% health with 185.0 projected FPPG unavailable. Even with a 24.7% season injury burden and 299 total injury games to navigate, Garrett has the best combination of bracket leverage, recent scoring form, and matchup advantage.

Hayden enters the playoffs as a great contradiction. Big Nik Energy finished the regular season dead-last at 5-16, yet still carries a 21.4% championship chance because the current roster is 88.5% healthy, has a 56-to-55 schedule edge on Nick, and still runs through Nikola Jokic. The long view still looks brutal: Hayden's 26.6% season injury burden and 317 total injury games were the league's worst. But the short view is suddenly excellent, and win or lose in the playoffs, Hayden's offseason is already loaded because he owns both his own 2026 1st-round pick and Garrett's 2026 1st-round pick, giving him the first two selections in the 2026 draft order.

That leaves Nick as the last team left on the board. Luka my Balls finished 17-4, led the league in points, and clinched the regular-season crown back in Week 18, yet his odds at the championship are now dead last in the league at 14.6%. That is not a contradiction so much as a format change: Nick still has the league's lowest season injury burden at 19.7% and is averaging 1822.4 over the last 3 weeks, but Hayden is healthier right now at the top of the bracket and Benton's side is giving Garrett a cleaner runway. Benton, meanwhile, is stuck in between; the No. 2 seed still owns 16.8% championship odds, but a 4-game losing streak, 25.9% season injury burden, and five major absences have turned Smaxey from contender into long shot.

Top Available Free Agents

Player NBA Team Position Proj FPPG Games This Week Games Next Week
Darius Garland LAC PG 37.06 4 3
Jalen Williams OKC SF,PF 34.68 4 3
Jrue Holiday POR PG,SG 34.53 4 2
Payton Pritchard BOS PG 34.07 3 4
Paul George PHI SG,SF,PF 33.74 3 4

Luka and Jokic still sit exactly where you would expect at the top of the season leaderboards, but Week 21 also produced the funniest possible snapshot of Josh Giddey: one of the five best single-game performances of the week and one of the five worst. Nickeil Alexander-Walker headlines the waiver table after dropping 163.05 FP in four games, which is a brutal little detail for everyone who let Nick patch a contender at the wire in the final week. The bottom tables are a graveyard of injured stars and partial seasons, with Jayson Tatum, Dejounte Murray, Trae Young, Domantas Sabonis, and Anthony Davis all sitting near the very bottom in total accumulation for very different reasons. And with playoffs now here, the free-agent board is less about long-term value than short-term survival, which makes names like Darius Garland, Jalen Williams, and Jrue Holiday feel even more conspicuous at the top.

3. 299 Garrett (2025-26, 169 in starter slots + 130 IL, 24.7% burden)
4. 237 Nick (2025-26, 144 in starter slots + 93 IL, 19.7% burden)

Around the NBA

  • Luka Drops 60 in Miami, but Nick Still Waits on Anthony Davis
    Luka Doncic's 60-point eruption in the Lakers' March 19 win at Miami was the loudest real-NBA performance tied to this league all week, and it matched what Week 21 looked like in fantasy: 257.35 FP across 4 games, with an 89.75 FP spike in that same Heat game. That kind of ceiling is the only reason Nick can walk into the semifinals with a real puncher's chance even while the bracket math treats the No. 1 seed like an underdog. The catch is that Washington announced Friday that Anthony Davis still needs more time to heal and will be re-evaluated at the end of March, so Nick's path is getting narrower, not wider. The playoffs start now, and Luka is going to have to keep doing the heavy lifting.
  • Jokic Closes the Regular Season With Yet Another Triple-Double
    Nikola Jokic recorded his 35th triple-double of the season Sunday in Denver's win over Portland, another reminder that Hayden's 5-16 record has almost nothing to do with the quality of his best player. In Week 21 fantasy terms, Jokic still put up 201.15 FP over 4 games and closed with a 66.90 FP line against Portland. That is why Hayden can limp out of the regular season and still walk into the bracket favored over Nick. In a two-week playoff sprint, a normal Jokic week can swing the entire league.
  • Wembanyama's Buzzer-Beater Sends San Antonio to the Playoffs
    Victor Wembanyama's game-winner against Phoenix on March 19 capped a 34-point, 12-rebound night and officially punched San Antonio's postseason ticket. Garrett already knew the fantasy version of that story: Wembanyama led Saboner with 189.60 FP in Week 21 and once again looked like the kind of player who can carry a matchup by himself. That timing matters because Garrett enters Week 22 with the best championship odds in the league despite finishing the regular season under .500. If Wemby keeps playing like this, the seed line next to Saboner's name stops meaning much.
  • Cade Cunningham's Collapsed Lung Turns Benton's Slide Into a Crisis
    Detroit announced Thursday that Cade Cunningham has a collapsed lung and will be re-evaluated in two weeks, a brutal real-NBA development for one of the league's biggest breakout stars. In fantasy terms, Benton was already feeling it: Cunningham missed two games in Week 21, Smaxey lost 11 games to injury overall, and the regular-season finale ended in a 59.95-point loss to Nick. Benton's four-game losing streak now rolls straight into the semifinals without the player who has been one of the roster's core pillars all year. The standings reset in the playoffs, but injury timing like this does not.
  • Curry Moves Closer, but Giannis Keeps Garrett's Injury Board Complicated
    Golden State said Saturday that Stephen Curry is progressing and should return to full team practices in the coming days, which at least gives Garrett a path to getting one of his biggest pieces back soon. That is the encouraging side of the ledger. The darker side is Milwaukee's update on Giannis Antetokounmpo: a hyperextended knee and bone bruise, with ESPN reporting the Bucks wanted to shut him down for the rest of the season even as Giannis pushed to return. Garrett can survive one missing star when Wembanyama is humming, but two absent headliners is exactly how a favorite gets dragged into a rock fight.

Rumor Mill

Trade Ideas

  • Hayden sends Kon Knueppel + Paolo Banchero -> Garrett sends Victor Wembanyama
    This is the biggest 2-for-1 swing on the board. Hayden would be trying to pair Jokic with another true franchise centerpiece, while Garrett would be turning one monster asset into two strong long-term pieces and more lineup flexibility. Garrett and Hayden have completed only 3 deals all time, so this would be rare blockbuster territory, but Hayden's grip on the top two picks in 2026 gives him enough future insulation to think big.
  • Garrett sends Giannis Antetokounmpo + Hayden's 2026 Round 4 pick -> Nick sends Amen Thompson
    This is pure offseason buy-low logic. Garrett would be cashing some name value plus a real mid-round asset he actually owns, while Nick would be betting that Giannis can still anchor one more title push around Luka if the knee situation calms down. Nick and Garrett have made 6 all-time deals, so there is enough history here for a player-plus-pick framework to feel plausible.
  • Garrett sends Cooper Flagg -> Hayden sends Scottie Barnes
    This is the cleanest buy-low idea in the file. Garrett would be turning rookie-name value into a player whose slump has dragged him 19.9% below projection over the last 4 weeks, while Hayden could decide that a longer runway with Flagg fits even better now that he already controls both of the first two picks in 2026. When a manager has that much draft capital, timeline bets get easier to make.
  • Benton sends Donovan Mitchell + Trey Murphy III -> Hayden sends Nikola Jokic
    The engine clearly likes the 2-for-1 logic, but this is still the hardest deal here to picture actually happening. Benton would be trying to compress value into one playoff-proof superstar after limping into Week 22, while Hayden would only consider it if he wanted more breadth around Shai and LaMelo. Benton and Hayden have completed 5 all-time deals, so the channel exists, but moving Jokic when you already hold both top-two picks would take an enormous overpay.

Free Agent Targets

  • Jalen Williams (SF,PF) -> Garrett: 34.68 proj FPPG. Addresses Garrett's injury-depleted F corps (34.7 FPPG). If he somehow stays loose for next year, this is exactly the kind of two-way wing Garrett should be monitoring around Wembanyama.
  • Reed Sheppard (PG,SG) -> Nick: 32.33 proj FPPG. Addresses Nick's injury-depleted G corps (32.3 FPPG, 8 games next 2 weeks). That is the kind of backcourt depth swing a top seed should keep on the offseason radar.
  • Kyle Filipowski (PF,C) -> Nick: 28.56 proj FPPG. Addresses Nick's injury-depleted C corps (28.6 FPPG, 8 games next 2 weeks). He fits the kind of frontcourt depth stash that matters once the summer reshapes rosters.
  • Payton Pritchard (PG) -> Benton: 34.07 proj FPPG. Addresses Benton's injury-depleted G corps (34.1 FPPG). With Cade and Maxey uncertainty hanging over the roster, this is a sensible stabilizer target.
  • Jakob Poeltl (C) -> Benton: 27.06 proj FPPG. Addresses Benton's injury-depleted C corps (27.1 FPPG, 8 games next 2 weeks). Benton needs playable center depth more than almost anyone.

Hot Streaks

  • Matas Buzelis (Hayden): 40.1% above projection over the last 4 weeks, and still 40.1% above projection over the last 14 days. Sell high candidate - actual 42.0 FPPG unlikely to sustain. Hayden has no reason to force a move during the playoff push, but that is exactly the kind of inflated summer value worth testing.
  • Josh Giddey (Benton): 28.4% above projection over the last 4 weeks, 36.5% above over the last 14 days. Sell high candidate - actual 51.2 FPPG unlikely to sustain. Because the short-term trend is even hotter, this one may still have a little more room to run.
  • Bam Adebayo (Benton): 24.9% above projection over the last 4 weeks, 36.1% above over the last 14 days. Elevated trade value - producing 48.3 FPPG vs 38.7 projected. He is trending up, which makes him just as defensible to hold as he is to shop.
  • Donovan Clingan (Garrett): 24.4% above projection over the last 4 weeks, 39.8% above over the last 14 days. Elevated trade value - producing 41.4 FPPG vs 33.2 projected. For a Garrett roster already built around size, this surge only makes the offseason calculus more interesting.
  • Kon Knueppel (Hayden): 17.6% above projection over the last 4 weeks, 16.9% above over the last 14 days. Elevated trade value - producing 33.8 FPPG vs 28.8 projected. The line says the heater has cooled a touch, so the sell window may be strongest right now.
  • OG Anunoby (Nick): 17.5% above projection over the last 4 weeks, but only 7.6% above over the last 14 days. Elevated trade value - producing 34.5 FPPG vs 29.4 projected. This is the clearest cooling-off profile in the group, which makes him more shop-him than ride-him.

Slump Watch

  • RJ Barrett (Hayden): 24.9% below projection over the last 4 weeks, and 24.9% below over the last 14 days. Replacement: Jalen Williams (34.68 FPPG). Consider Williams as the cleaner upside play if Barrett's market still carries any name-value juice.
  • Miles Bridges (Nick): 19.0% below projection over the last 4 weeks, and 19.0% below over the last 14 days. Replacement: Jalen Williams (34.68 FPPG). If Nick wants to turn a shaky depth slot into a cleaner keeper profile, that is the obvious template.
  • Giannis Antetokounmpo (Garrett): 22.3% below projection over the last 4 weeks, and 23.2% below over the last 14 days. No clear replacement available. This is a health bet, not a talent debate, so the move is hold and reassess once the knee situation settles.
  • Julius Randle (Benton): 21.4% below projection over the last 4 weeks, but 16.8% below over the last 14 days. No clear replacement available. Trending up -> hold for now.
  • Scottie Barnes (Hayden): 19.9% below projection over the last 4 weeks, but 13.7% below over the last 14 days. No clear replacement available. Trending up -> hold for now.
  • Alperen Sengun (Garrett): 18.4% below projection over the last 4 weeks, and 20.2% below over the last 14 days. No clear replacement available. The dip is getting worse, which is exactly why a buy-low caller will show up.
End of Newsletter
CHS Alumni Fantasy Basketball League
